video_title: Why Russia is Suddenly Losing in Ukraine
transcript: Something interesting is starting to happen in Ukraine right now. For the first time in years since the early days of the Russian invasion, it now appears that the Ukrainians are regaining the momentum again. And that the Russian war machine along with the economy that supports it are both beginning to crack under the enormous strains of a full-scale war that is now well into its fifth year. To put the sluggish pace of Russia's recent military advances into perspective, consider that in the first phase of the war in 2014, the Russians seized control over about 7% of Ukraine's territory. Then, in the opening weeks of the full-scale invasion in early 2022, the Russians managed to expand that to an occupation of nearly 27% of Ukraine's territory at the peak before successful Ukrainian counteroffensives managed to reduce that down to about 18% of Ukraine's territory by November of 2022. Over the past three and a half years of brutal, grinding, attritional warfare since that time, the Russians have only managed to seize control over another 1.5% of Ukraine's territory and have paid an almost incalculable price in blood to do so. More than 1 million Russian soldiers have been killed or wounded just in order to conquer this extra 1.5% of Ukraine's land during these past 3 and 1/2 years. To put this into perspective for my fellow Americans, the Russians have spent three and a half years and have paid more than 1 million casualties just in order to conquer an area of land that is smaller than Los Angeles County in Southern California. When adding the casualties from the early days of the invasion before November of 2022, total Russian casualties, both killed and wounded in the war, have by now likely climbed higher than 1.2 2 million with British and Swedish intelligence both now estimating that around 500,000 of those Russian casualties are deaths. In other words, around 3% of Russia's entire pre-war male population of fighting age has been either killed or wounded by this war in Ukraine. An utterly devastating statistic for any society that seems like it should belong in the 20th century rather than in the 21st. And the pace of Russia's casualties have only been increasing. Since the start of 2026, at least according to Ukrainian sources, Russia has been averaging roughly 35,000 casualties on the front lines in Ukraine every single month, which has been starting to outpace Russia's ability to find new recruits to replace them. This is a massive gamecher in the war if it's true. Because throughout 2024 and most of 2025, Russia was still able to recruit more men for the war than it was losing. Russia was able to do this by offering up huge financial incentives for enlisting, which has been particularly attractive in Russia's less developed poor rural regions. Soldiers enlisting could earn up to seven or eight times the median annual Russian wage during just one year of service on the front. On top of generous debt forgiveness, free college tuition, and even free land handouts. Throughout 2025, when Russia was averaging closer to 23,000 casualties a month, these incentives were strong enough to always find more than enough replacements. But now in 2026, with a much higher casualty ratio of 35,000 a month, they haven't been enough. And for the first time in this war, Russia is failing to replace all of their losses in manpower. Ukraine's manpower losses have been numerically smaller, but even more catastrophic as a demographic percentage. The Center for Strategic and International Studies believes that Ukrainian casualties, both killed and wounded throughout the war, now number somewhere around 600,000. This is only about half of the total casualties suffered by the Russians. But it also represents nearly 7% of Ukraine's entire pre-war fighting age male population that have been killed or wounded, compared to Russia's 3%. This in a nutshell has been the brutal logic of Russia's attritional warfare strategy that they adopted against Ukraine in November of 2022. Russia's larger manpower and resources meant that Russia could pay much higher casualties in a numerical sense. While Ukraine was slowly ground down by much higher demographic damage, Russia's bet was that eventually the higher demographic damage that was being inflicted upon Ukraine through this style of attritional warfare would cause them to break first and result in a rapid collapse of their front lines all at once. Which is why Russia has felt comfortable with their ability to afford paying numerically higher casualty rates until then. But the problem for the Kremlin is that the longer this war continues going on and the longer that Ukraine stubbornly holds out, the more damage it's still causing to Russia, too. For an increasingly impatient population that has been repeatedly promised a quick and decisive victory. In January of 2026, what was initially envisioned as being simply a 3-day special military operation passed an ominous milestone, the 1,418 day mark, exceeding the length of the Soviet Union's entire war against Nazi Germany. And if Russia is to achieve even its most minimum of objectives during this war, it is likely to last for many years longer. Still, if Russia is able to maintain the same pace of advance it achieved in 2024 and 2025, they will still not be capable of completing the conquest of the rest of the eastern Donbass region for another 2 or 3 years, pushing the war well into 2028 or even 2029. Full control over the entire Donbass region is the top priority war objective of the Kremlins that they have never been willing to compromise on. and Putin has ordered the Russian military to finish it by the end of this year in 2026. In order to do so, the Russian military will have to begin advancing much more rapidly than they were able to do in 2024 and 2025. But it is significantly more heavily defended and reinforced urban Ukrainian defenses. And worse for the Kremlin, since April of this year, the Russians have actually started losing ground in the war for the first time in years. In April, the Russians suffered a net loss of 116 square km of territory in Ukraine, marking the first monthly net territorial loss that the Kremlin's forces have suffered in Ukraine since the major Ukrainian counteroffens offensive 3 years previously in 2023. The Russians have lost even further ground in May, and the comparisons with their military performance in 2025 are striking. According to the Institute for the Study of War, Russian forces seized control over roughly 516 km of territory in Ukraine between December 2024 and May 2025. While over the same period this year, they managed to lose 280 km. Russia's advances nearly everywhere along the front line of ground to a virtual halt over these past couple of months. And so the idea that Russia could take over the entire Dawnbass in this environment by 2028, let alone by the end of 2026 as ordered by Putin, seems very far-fetched. So why is this happening now? Why is Russia's army seemingly running out of steam and beginning to lose ground? And how much weaker is Russia than they actually seem to appear? First, Russia's economy is probably not doing as well as official Russian government data would suggest. The Russian government claims that the country's GDP expanded by about 13% in the years between 2020 and 2024 despite the war. However, in a recent op-ed that was published in the New York Times by the foreign minister of Sweden, Maria Malder Stenergard, she wrote that a Swedish government analysis of the change in Russia's nighttime luminosity suggested that the Russian economy actually contracted by 8% during that time span instead. She also pointed out that while Russia's official inflation figure in 2024 was only 10%, the Russian central bank still hiked their interest rates to 21% the same year as well, suggesting that the inflation problem in Russia is significantly worse than the official figures would imply. Currently, in mid 2026, Russia claims that its inflation figure is only 5.2%. But Sweden's military intelligence chief has estimated that in reality it's closer to 15%. three times higher than the officially reported figure. If that's accurate, then Russia's current capacity for military spending is much weaker than it actually appears. And these inflationary demons in Russia aren't likely to go away anytime soon. With a sharp demographic downturn and a high demand for manpower in both the military and the defense industrial base, these inflationary pressures on Russia will inevitably continue persisting so long as the war goes on and will be negatively impacting the Russian civilian population that has to deal with it. As the war goes on, with no promised victory in sight, the casualties piling up, the inflation persisting, and state repression intensifying through increased internet and media censorship, the approval rating of Putin's government is beginning to tumble. Even according to official Russian stateowned data, which indicates that Putin's current approval rating in Russia has fallen down to 65.6%. A significant decrease from the 77.8% that he enjoyed immediately before the start of the invasion. and well below the 80% plus ratings that he was getting for years before then. And of course, many new Ukrainian strategies that they've adopted since mid2025 have also been contributing to these negative outcomes for Russia, too, because they're working exceptionally well. Over a year ago, in March of 2025, Zalinsky released a video in which he discussed a new strategy of bringing the war back to Russia. In it, he said, quote, "The war was brought from Russia, and it is to Russia that the war must be pushed back. They must be the ones forced into peace. They are the ones who must be pressured to ensure security." End quote. Since that time, Ukraine has largely halted their older strategy of conducting costly ground counter offensives to reclaim territory that was always very casualty inensive and have adopted a more long range asymmetric strategy that is designed to harm the Russian economy and civilian morale on the home front instead. After Russia conquered the city of Abdiva on the outskirts of Donetsk in early 2024, Ukraine was forced onto the defensive and faced crippling shortages of artillery shells and delays in US military aid that it had been expecting. Forced to innovate, Ukraine increasingly turned to small FPV drones as a cheap and effective substitute for their lack of conventional artillery. FPV drone production rapidly increased to the point where now more than 150 local Ukrainian manufacturers are building millions of new FPV drones a year and hundreds of thousands of them are now being fielded along the front line at any given time. Russia has responded with similar levels of FPV drone production and now the entire front line is completely saturated with these modern weapons of war. for 745 miles along the entire length of the front lines stretching between 15 and 30 m deep. Now, the density of these short range FPV drones have established a kill zone that is nearly impossible for either sign to penetrate. Just about every square inch of this kill zone is under constant drone surveillance, full of buzzing kill drones armed with explosives. This environment has made the mass armored breakthroughs that define 20th century warfare virtually impossible today and has been dramatically contributing to the rise in battlefield casualties and deaths for both sides. Though it is currently affecting the Russians more since they're the ones who are still largely attempting to advance through it. The constant presence of explosive drones within the kill zone makes medical evacuations for wounded soldiers with helicopters or ambulances almost impossible. Which means that wounded soldiers are being forced to go untreated for hours or even days. As a result, the ratio of wounded to killed soldiers has gone from 10 to one near the start of the war down to three or 4:1 instead a day. Today, drones are believed to account for around 70% of all Russian and Ukrainian casualties on the battlefield, and both sides are finding it difficult to break through the other's drone wall. And combined with these swarms of short-range FPV drones along the front line that typically have ranges of less than 100 km, the Ukrainians are combining increased usage of more advanced intermediate range drones to strike Russian logistics and longrange drones to strike against the Russian economy. The intermediate range drones have ranges of up to 300 km. And Ukraine's production of these types of drones is five times greater in 2026 than it was in 2025, according to Zalinski. And the Ukrainians have been making good use of them in 2026 by striking against Russian logistics and support infrastructure far behind the front lines like roads, ammunition depots, staging areas, anti-air systems, radars, and drone warehouses among other targets. This year, the M14 highway, which extends from the Russian city of Rustavon through occupied Maria Poland on into Crimea, has come under particularly heavy attack by these Ukrainian intermediate range drones. Open source analysts on X have mapped out more than 125 intermediate range drone strikes along this highway over just the past couple of months alone, which have destroyed dozens and dozens of Russian trucks transporting ammunition, fuel, spare parts, and reinforcements to the front line. All of this is hindering Russia's ability to adequately resupply their own front lines. While some Russian military bloggers like Vladimir Romanov have even claimed that they are contributing to fuel shortages in Crimean cities like Sevastapole. Building anti- drone countermeasures along highways like this like drone nets takes precious time to do. While Russian logistics can't simply just stop using the highways in the meantime. On the 27th of May, Ukraine's 412th Nemesis Brigade reported that these intermediate strikes have already forced Russia into restricting heavy military traffic along sections of the R280 corridor that links the occupying cities of Maria and Malita with Crimea, which is undermining Russia's ability to resupply its entire southern front. And then on top of these intermediate and short-range drone strikes that are impairing Russian logistics and combat operations, the Ukrainians are increasingly using long range drone and missile strikes to impair the Russian economy that funds the war effort as well. With ranges punching as deep as 1,750 km into Russia proper, these long range Ukrainian strikes are now penetrating 2 and 1/2 times as far as they were when the war started 4 years ago. There are many places that Ukraine has been targeting with these long range strikes, but they've particularly been striking Russia's oil and gas infrastructure and maritime ports in a calculated effort to eat into as much of Russia's oil and gas revenue streams as possible. After the US and Israel launched a war against Iran in 2026 and Iran began blocking the straight of Hormuz, the global price of oil skyrocketed, which led to widespread expectations that Russia, who was among the world's largest producers and exporters of oil, would reap a huge windfall that would help fund its military. However, just across April and May of this year, the Ukrainians managed to strike at least 20 Russian oil refineries and export terminals with these kind of long range strikes. Most dramatic of all was in April when long-range Ukrainian drones managed to strike the Talbs oil refinery and export terminal on Russia's Black Sea coast. The attack caused a massive oil spill that contaminated at least 50 kilometers of the Russian coast. Thick black smoke from the fire stretched all the way down south to the resort town of Sochi that was even visible to airline passengers while rains saturated with oil and soot fell around the city and contaminated its soil. Ukraine has been able to conduct strikes virtually all across western Russia this year from St. Petersburg to Moscow to the Caucasus and nearly as far east as the Eurals. And as a result of all of this, Bloomberg has reported that Russia's seabour oil exports fell by roughly 300,000 barrels per day in March. While the average output at all Russian oil refineries fell to 4.69 69 million barrels per day total in April, which is ominously the lowest amount of oil production that Russia has managed since December of 2009. Russia's much anticipated windfall from the spike in oil prices that was caused by the Iran war so far has also been heavily countered by these concentrated long range Ukrainian attacks, which by proxy has negatively affected Russia's ability to continue paying for the war. Ukraine has dubbed these kinds of attacks against Russian oil and gas infrastructure as a sort of kinetic sanctions. While Russian oil and gas companies all across the country have been forced into investing in expensive counter drone defenses like signal jammers, giant metal nets, or even anti-aircraft systems. Then an additional factor that's been complicating Russian war efforts has been SpaceX's decision in February of this year to block off the Russians access to their Starlink satellite network within the occupied areas of Ukraine. The Russian military had reportedly smuggled in loads of unauthorized Starlink internet terminals in the occupied territories of Ukraine that they were using to facilitate their battlefield communications and drone operations. The Ukrainian government worked together with SpaceX to get all of these unauthorized Starling terminals deactivated, which has only added on even further pressure on the Russians, frustrating their battlefield communications and forcing Russian drone operators to switch back to using more visible antennas again. The Ukrainian counter drone operators can more easily spawn and destroy. And then there's the Russian government's own throttling of the Telegram messenger app as well. For those of you who might not know, Telegram is the most popular messenger app of choice across most of the former Soviet block countries like Russia, Ukraine, and Belleras. However, Russia began partially throttling and blocking Telegram in May of 2025. And since April of 2026, they've been largely blocking it completely. The Russian government has claimed that the block of telegram is necessary to prevent Ukrainian drones from accessing local mobile networks. But it has also conveniently for them shut down protest groups ability to effectively organize. And it is forcing Russian civilians to adopt state controlled alternatives where their communications can be more effectively monitored instead. Since Russian soldiers and officers have also been heavily relying upon Telegram for their own battlefield communications and logistics coordination, the block has also frustrated them even further. on top of the Starling ban and the Ukrainian drone strikes. And then in April, Hungary's longtime authoritarian prime minister Victor Orban lost his re-election bid in a landslide defeat to his rival Peter Majar. This was critical for Ukraine because for months, Orban's government was vetoing and blocking a 90 billion euro loan package to Ukraine that the rest of the EU was attempting to pass. With Orban's decisive defeat in the election, his incoming replacement, Peter Majar, has promised that he will immediately remove Hungary's veto on the loan package, which means that Ukraine will be receiving a 90 billion euro loan injection from the EU to continue staying in the fight for longer. The loan is also interest free for Ukraine, while Brussels believes that the entire principal amount will eventually be fully repaid back to them through reparation payments from Russia. Meanwhile, Ukraine is also solidifying their defense relationships with the Western world as well, which is in a sense ensuring continued Western support for their cause, even in the absence of formal defense agreements. Germany and Ukraine have signed a deal to jointly produce thousands of AI enabled intermediate range drones out of a factory in Bavaria. The UK is jointly producing combat drones with Ukraine and Sulk. And Denmark is jointly producing rocket fuel with Ukraine and Jutland. Most notably of all, after Iranian drones embarrassed American anti-air defenses in the Persian Gulf when they destroyed more than $1.3 billion dollars in US military hardware for just the price of a few Toyota Corollas. The US decided to bring in 201 Ukrainian anti- drone specialists to Saudi Arabia to help with shooting them down. The more that Ukraine innovates in the war against Russia, the more experience and technology they'll have to share with the rest of the world for the wars of tomorrow. And that alone is making it far less likely that Ukraine's allies will ever abandon them. Altogether, Ukraine's strategies are starting to work in 2026. The tide of battle is tangibly beginning to shift, and Russia is running out of good options for accomplishing even their most minimum of objectives in the war, like finalizing the occupation of the Donbass region by the end of this year. Because of all of these setbacks the Ukrainians have managed to inflict on the Russians this year, Putin is likely going to be left with only two highly unpalatable choices. He could settle for an attempt at a ceasefire roughly along the current front lines and abandon the stated objective of securing the entire dawn bass. But Ukraine might not be willing to entertain that if they're the ones continuing to gain more momentum. While hardline Russians who expect a victory in the war would likely be outraged at the sense of betrayal and half measures. On the other hand, Putin could also still choose to escalate the war further by ordering up another general mobilization round that could forcibly deploy another 200,000 more Russian soldiers to the front lines. But doing that would be extremely politically risky for the Kremlin, especially as Putin's own polling numbers have been significantly declining this year. The last and so far only time that Putin has ordered a general mobilization during this war was back in September of 2022 for 300,000 men in the early days of the invasion and it led to more than a million Russian men of fighting age fleeing the country to avoid service and was hugely unpopular. With the Russian economy showing huge signs of stress that the government is not publicly acknowledging with the war already in its fifth year that has already been Russia's bloodiest conflict since the Second World War with internet censorship and Ukrainian drone strikes across the country both increasing with the army stalled out on the front lines with a prior history of military mutinies like the great Fogna group rebellion over the summer of 2023 and with Hudin's own popularity in Russia already at a historic low point. Would Putin actually risk it all on another mobilization order to try and finish the occupation of the Dawn Bass? Or would he attempt to settle for some kind of a ceasefire? Time will tell, but for now, 2026 is increasingly looking like it'll be Ukraine's year. Ultimately though, figuring out and solving the geopolitical crisis in Ukraine will likely be as difficult going forward as it's already been for more than a decade since Russian troops first marched across the border and seized Crimea in 2014. But something that is very much figured out on the other hand is what you should watch next or at least put on your list and that's the China that she grew up in.
